title: Dog Whining or Crying While Urinating: Causes & Next Steps
meta_desc: Is your dog crying while peeing? Learn about urinary pain, urgency levels, and why photos help your vet diagnose the issue quickly.
content_html:
TL;DR: If your dog cries while urinating, it indicates they are in pain and requires a veterinary visit within 24 hours; however, if they are straining without producing any urine, it is a life-threatening emergency.

What causes my dog to cry or whine while they are urinating?

When a dog whines or cries while attempting to urinate, it is a clinical sign known as dysuria. This vocalization indicates that the act of passing urine is causing physical pain or significant discomfort. Common causes include urinary tract infections (UTIs), bladder stones, crystals in the urine, or inflammation of the bladder or urethra.

Is it a veterinary emergency if my dog is in pain while peeing?

  • The urgency level for this condition is Medium.
  • While painful urination requires prompt veterinary attention, it is usually not a life-threatening emergency unless the dog is unable to produce any urine at all.
  • If your dog is straining without results, this is a blockage and constitutes an immediate emergency.
  • Otherwise, seek a veterinary appointment within 24 hours to address the underlying cause of the pain.

How can a photo or video of my dog's urine help with the triage process?

  • Capturing a photo of the urine on a light-colored surface or in a clear container can be invaluable for triage.
  • A photo allows a veterinary professional to see the color, clarity, and presence of blood (hematuria) or sediment.
  • A short video of the dog attempting to urinate can help the vet distinguish between straining to pee and straining to defecate, ensuring an accurate diagnosis.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs, painful or prolonged attempts at urination (stranguria) may manifest as whining or crying. This can be associated with partial or complete urethral obstruction, commonly caused by uroliths. Irritation and inflammation at the site of urolith lodgement contribute to urethral occlusion. Partial obstruction may result in dribbling of blood-tinged urine, while complete obstruction can lead to tenesmus, tail twitching, and weight shifting. Urge incontinence associated with cystitis, hormonal-responsive urethral incompetence, and paradoxical urinary incontinence due to partial urethral obstruction may also contribute to these clinical signs.

Chapter: Urology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1526)

faqs.question: Can a dog's diet cause painful urination?
faqs.answer: Yes, certain diets can contribute to the formation of bladder stones or crystals, which irritate the urinary tract and cause pain during urination.

faqs.question: Are female dogs more prone to urinary issues?
faqs.answer: Female dogs are generally more susceptible to bacterial urinary tract infections due to their anatomy, though male dogs are at higher risk for dangerous urethral blockages.

faqs.question: What should I do if my dog stops producing urine entirely?
faqs.answer: If your dog is straining but nothing is coming out, seek emergency veterinary care immediately, as this may indicate a life-threatening obstruction.
